the last meeting at matching volume i chapter i ferdinand lopez it be certainly of service to a man to know who be his grandfather and who be his grandmother if he entertain an ambition to move in the upper circle of society and also of service to be able to speak of they as of person who be themselves somebody in their time
no doubt we all entertain great respect for those who by their own energy have raise themselves in the world and when we hear that the son of a washerwoman have become lord chancellor or archbishop of canterbury we do theoretically and abstractedly feel a high reverence for such self-make magnate than for one who have be as it be bear into forensic or ecclesiastical purple
but not the less must the offspring of the washerwoman have have very much trouble on the subject of his birth unless he have be when young as well as when old a very great man indeed
after the goal have be absolutely reach and the honour and the title and the wealth actually win a man may talk with some humour even with some affection of the maternal tub but while the struggle be go on with the conviction strong upon the struggler that he cannot be altogether successful unless he be esteem a gentleman not to be ashamed not to conceal the old family circumstance not at any rate to be silent be difficult
and the difficulty be certainly not less if fortunate circumstance rather than hard work and intrinsic merit have raise above his natural place an aspirant to high social position
can it be expect that such a one when din with a duchess shall speak of his father s small shop or bring into the light of day his grandfather s cobbler s awl
and yet it be difficult to be altogether silent
it may not be necessary for any of we to be always talk of our own parentage
we may be generally reticent as to our uncle and aunt and may drop even our brother and sister in our ordinary conversation
but if a man never mention his belonging among those with whom he live he become mysterious and almost open to suspicion
it begin to be know that nobody know anything of such a man and even friend become afraid
it be certainly convenient to be able to allude if it be but once in a year to some blood relation
ferdinand lopez who in other respect have much in his circumstance on which to congratulate himself suffer trouble in his mind respect his ancestor such as i have endeavour to describe
he do not know very much himself but what little he do know he keep altogether to himself
he have no father or mother no uncle aunt brother or sister no cousin even whom he could mention in a cursory way to his dear friend
he suffer no doubt but with spartan consistency he so hide his trouble from the world that no one know that he suffer
those with whom he live and who speculate often and wonder much as to who he be never dream that the silent man s reticence be a burden to himself
at no special conjuncture of his life at no period which could be mark with the finger of the observer do he glaringly abstain from any statement which at the moment might be natural
he never hesitate blush or palpably labour at concealment but the fact remain that though a great many man and not a few woman know ferdinand lopez very well none of they know whence he have come or what be his family
he be a man however naturally reticent who never allude to his own affair unless in pursuit of some object the way to which be clear before his eye
silence therefore on a matter which be common in the mouth of most man be less difficult to he than to another and the result less embarrassing
dear old jones who tell his friend at the club of every pound that he lose or win at the race who boast of mary s favour and mourn over lucy s coldness almost in public who issue bulletin on the state of his purse his stomach his stable and his debt could not with any amount of care keep from we the fact that his father be an attorney s clerk and make his first money by discount small bill
everybody know it and jones who like popularity grieve at the unfortunate publicity
but jones be relieve from a burden which would have break his poor shoulder and which even ferdinand lopez who be a strong man often find it hard to bear without wince
it be admit on all side that ferdinand lopez be a gentleman johnson say that any other derivation of this difficult word than that which cause it to signify a man of ancestry be whimsical
there be many who in define the term for their own use still adhere to johnson s dictum but they adhere to it with certain unexpressed allowance for possible exception
the chance be very much in favour of the well-bear man but exception may exist
it be not generally believe that ferdinand lopez be well born but he be a gentleman
and this most precious rank be accede to he although he be employed or at least have be employed on business which do not of itself give such a warrant of position as be suppose to be afford by the bar and the church by the military service and by physic
he have be on the stock exchange and still in some manner not clearly understand by his friend do business in the city
at the time with which we be now concerned ferdinand lopez be thirty-three year old and as he have begin life early he have be long before the world
it be know of he that he have be at a good english private school and it be report on the solitary evidence of one who have there be his schoolfellow that a rumour be current in the school that his school bill be pay by an old gentleman who be not relate to he
thence at the age of seventeen he have be send to a german university and at the age of twenty-one have appear in london in a stockbroker s office where he be soon know as an accomplished linguist and as a very clever fellow precocious not give to many pleasure apt for work but hardly trustworthy by employer not as be dishonest but as have a taste for be a master rather than a servant
indeed his period of servitude be very short
it be not in his nature to be active on behalf of other
he be soon active for himself and at one time it be suppose that he be make a fortune
then it be know that he have leave his regular business and it be suppose that he have lose all that he have ever make or have ever possess
but nobody not even his own banker or his own lawyer not even the old woman who look after his linen ever really know the state of his affair
he be certainly a handsome man his beauty being of a sort which man be apt to deny and woman to admit lavishly
he be nearly six foot tall very dark and very thin with regular well-cut feature indicate little to the physiognomist unless it be the great gift of self-possession
his hair be cut short and he wear no beard beyond an absolutely black moustache
his tooth be perfect in form and whiteness a characteristic which though it may be a value item in a general catalogue of personal attraction do not generally recommend a man to the unconscious judgment of his acquaintance
but about the mouth and chin of this man there be a something of softness perhaps in the play of the lip perhaps in the dimple which in some degree lessen the feeling of hardness which be produce by the square brow and bold unflinche combative eye
they who know he and like he be reconcile by the low face
the great number who know he and do not like he feel and resented even though in nine case out of ten they might express no resentment even to themselves the pugnacity of his steady glance
for he be essentially one of those man who be always in the inner working of their mind defend themselves and attack other
he could not give a penny to a woman at a crossing without a look which argue at full length her injustice in make her demand and his freedom from all liability let he walk the crossing as often as he might
he could not seat himself in a railway carriage without a lesson to his opposite neighbour that in all the mutual affair of travelling arrangement of foot disposition of bag and opening of window it would be that neighbour s duty to submit and his to exact
it be however for the spirit rather than for the thing itself that he combat
the woman with the broom get her penny
the opposite gentleman when once by a glance he have express submission be allow his own way with his leg and with the window
i would not say that ferdinand lopez be prone to do ill-natured thing but he be imperious and he have learn to carry his empire in his eye
the reader must submit to be tell one or two furth and still small detail respect the man and then the man shall be allow to make his own way
no one of those around he know how much care he take to dress himself well or how careful he be that no one should know it
his very tailor regard he as be simply extravagant in the number of his coat and trouser and his friend look upon he as one of those fortunate being to whose nature belong a facility of be well dress or almost an impossibility of be ill dress
we all know the man a little man generally who move seldom and softly who look always as though he have just be send home in a bandbox
ferdinand lopez be not a little man and move freely enough but never at any moment going into the city or come out of it on horseback or on foot at home over his book or after the maze of the dance wa he dress otherwise than with perfect care
money and time do it but folk think that it grow with he as do his hair and his nail
and he always ride a horse which charm good judge of what a park nag should be not a prancing restless giggling sideway-go useless garran but an animal well make well bit with perfect pace on whom a rider if it please he could be as quiet as a statue on a monument
it often do please ferdinand lopez to be quiet on horseback and yet he do not look like a statue for it be acknowledge through all london that he be a good horseman
he live luxuriously too though whether at his ease or not nobody knew for he keep a brougham of his own and during the hunting season he have two horse down at leighton
there have once be a belief abroad that he be ruin but they who interest themselves in such matter have find out or at any rate believe that they have find out that he pay his tailor regularly and now there prevail an opinion that ferdinand lopez be a monied man
it be know to some few that he occupy room in a flat at westminster but to very few exactly where the room be situate
among all his friend no one be know to have enter they
in a moderate way he be give to hospitality that be to infrequent but when the occasion come to graceful hospitality
some club however or tavern or perhaps in the summer some river bank would be choose as the scene of these festivity
to a few if as suggest amidst summer flower on the water s edge to man and woman mixed he would be a courtly and efficient host for he have the rare gift of do such thing well
hunting be over and the east wind be still blow and a great portion of the london world be out of town take its easter holiday when on an unpleasant morning ferdinand lopez travel into the city by the metropolitan railway from westminster bridge
it be his custom to go thither when he do go not daily like a man of business but as chance might require like a capitalist or a man of pleasure in his own brougham
but on this occasion he walk down to the river side and then walk from the mansion house into a dingy little court call little tankard yard near the bank of england and go through a narrow dark long passage get into a little office at the back of a building in which there sit at a desk a greasy gentleman with a new hat on one side of his head who might perhaps be about forty year old
the place be very dark and the man be turn over the leaf of a ledger
a stranger to city way might probably have say that he be idle but he be no doubt fill his mind with that erudition which would enable he to earn his bread
on the other side of the desk there be a little boy copying letter
these be mr
sextus parker commonly call sexty parker and his clerk
mr
parker be a gentleman very well know and at the present moment favourably esteem on the stock exchange
what lopez say he
uncommon glad to see you
what can i do for you just come inside will you say lopez
now within mr
parker s very small office there be a small office in which there be a safe a small rickety pembroke table two chair and an old washing-stand with a tumbled towel
lopez lead the way into this sanctum as though he know the place well and sexty parker follow he
beastly day benot it say sexty
yes a nasty east wind cut one in two with a hot sun at the same time
one ought to hybernate at this time of the year then why donot you hybernate say lopez
business be too good
thatbe about it
a man have to stick to it when it do come
everybody cannot do like you give up regular work and make a well thing of an hour now and an hour then just as it please you
i shouldnot dare go in for that kind of thing i donot suppose you or any one else know what i go in for say lopez with a look that indicate offence
nor donot care say sexty only hope itbe something good for your sake sexty parker have know mr
lopez well now for some year and be an overbearing man himself somewhat even of a bully if the truth be spoken and by no mean apt to give way unless hard press have often try his hand on his friend as he himself would have say
but i doubt whether he could remember any instance in which he could congratulate himself on success
he be try his hand again now but do it with a falter voice have catch a glance of his friend s eye
i dare say not say lopez
then he continue without change his voice or the nature of the glance of his eye iwill tell you what i want you to do now
i want your name to this bill for three month sexty parker open his mouth and his eye and take the bit of paper that be tender to he
it be a promissory note for l750 which if sign by he would at the end of the specify period make he liable for that sum be it not otherwise pay
his friend mr
lopez be indeed apply to he for the assistance of his name in raise a loan to the amount of the sum name
this be a kind of favour which a man should ask almost on his knees and which if so ask mr
sextus parker would certainly refuse
and here be ferdinand lopez ask it whom sextus parker have latterly regard as an opulent man and ask it not at all on his knee but as one might say at the muzzle of a pistol
accommodation bill say sexty
why you benot hard up be you ibe not go just at present to tell you much about my affair and yet i expect you to do what i ask you
i donot suppose you doubt my ability to raise l750 oh dear no say sexty who have be look at and who have not borne the inspection well
and i donot suppose you would refuse i even if i be hard up as you call it there have be affair before between the two man in which lopez have probably be the strong and the memory of they add to the inspection which be still go on be heavy upon poor sexty
oh dear no i benot think of refuse
i suppose a fellow may be a little surprised at such a thing i donot know why you need be surprised as such thing be very common
i happen to have take a share in a loan a little beyond my immediate mean and therefore want a few hundred
there be no one i can ask with a well grace than you
if you ain t--afraid about it just sign it oh i benot afraid say sexty take his pen and write his name across the bill
but even before the signature be finish when his eye be take away from the face of his companion and fix upon the disagreeable piece of paper beneath his hand he repent of what he be do
he almost arrest his signature half-way
he do hesitate but have not pluck enough to stop his hand
it do seem to be a d----d odd transaction all the same he say as he lean back in his chair
itbe the commonest thing in the world say lopez pick up the bill in a leisurely way fold it and put it into his pocket-book
have our name never be together on a bit of paper before when we both have something to make by it youhave nothing to make and nothing to lose by this
good day and many thanks though i donot think so much of the affair as you seem to do then ferdinand lopez take his departure and sexty parker be leave alone in his bewilderment
by george that s queer he say to himself
whowould have think of lopez be hard up for a few hundred pound
but it must be all right
he wouldnot have come in that fashion if it havenot be all right
i oughtnot to have do it though
a man ought never to do that kind of thing never never and mr
sextus parker be much discontent with himself so that when he get home that evening to the wife of his bosom and his little family at ponders end he by no mean make himself agreeable to they
for that sum of l750 sit upon his bosom as he eat his supper and lie upon his chest as he slept like a nightmare
chapter ii everett wharton on that same day lopez din with his friend everett wharton at a new club call the progress of which they be both member
the progress be certainly a new club have as yet be open hardly more than three year but still it be old enough to have see many of the hope of its early youth become dim with age and inaction
for the progress have intend to do great thing for the liberal party or rather for political liberality in general and have in truth do little or nothing
it have be get up with considerable enthusiasm and for a while certain fiery politician have believe that through the instrumentality of this institution man of genius and spirit and natural power but without wealth meaning always themselves would be supply with sure seat in parliament and a probable share in the government
but no such result have be achieve
there have be a want of something some deficiency feel but not yet defined which have hitherto be fatal
the young man say it be because no old stager who know the way of pull the wire would come forward and put the club in the proper groove
the old man say it be because the young man be pretentious puppy
it be however not to be doubt that the party of progress have become slack and that the liberal politician of the country although a special new club have be open for the furtherance of their view be not at present make much way
what we want be organization say one of the lead young man
but the organization be not as yet forthcoming
the club nevertheless go on its way like other club and man din and smoke and play billiard and pretend to read
some few energetic member still hop that a good day would come in which their grand idea might be realised but as regard the member generally they be content to eat and drink and play billiard
it be a fairly good club with a sprinkling of liberal lordling a couple of dozen of member of parliament who have be make to believe that they would neglect their party duty unless they pay their money and the usual assortment of barrister attorney city merchant and idle man
it be good enough at any rate for ferdinand lopez who be particular about his dinner and have an opinion of his own about wine
he have be hear to assert that for real quiet comfort there be not a club in london equal to it but his hearer be not aware that in past day he have be blackball at the t---- and the g----
these be accident which lopez have a gift of keep in the background
his present companion everett wharton have as well as himself be an original member and wharton have be one of those who have hop to find in the club a step-stone to high political life and who now talk often with idle energy of the need of organization
for myself say lopez i can conceive no vainer object of ambition than a seat in the british parliament
what do any man gain by it
the few who be successful work very hard for little pay and no thanks or nearly equally hard for no pay and as little thank
the many who fail sit idly for hour undergo the weary task of listen to platitude and enjoy in return the now absolutely valueless privilege of have m p
write on their letter somebody must make law for the country i donot see the necessity
i think the country would do uncommonly well if it be to know that no old law would be alter or new law make for the next twenty year you wouldnot have repeal the corn law there be no corn law to repeal now nor modify the income tax i would modify nothing
but at any rate whether law be to be alter or to be leave it be a comfort to i that i need not put my finger into that pie
there be one benefit indeed in be in the house you cannot be arrest well that as far as it go and one other
it assist a man in get a seat as the director of certain company
people be still such ass that they trust a board of directors make up of member of parliament and therefore of course member be make welcome
but if you want to get into the house why donot you arrange it with your father instead of wait for what the club may do for you my father wouldnot pay a shilling for such a purpose
he be never in the house himself and therefore despise it a little of that perhaps
no man ever work hard than he do or in his way more successfully and have see one after another of his junior become member of parliament while he stick to the attorney there be perhaps a little jealousy about it from what i see of the way you live at home i should think your father would do anything for you with proper management
there be no doubt i suppose that he could afford it my father never in his life say anything to i about his own money affair though he say a great deal about mine
no man ever be close than my father
but i believe that he could afford almost anything i wish i have such a father say ferdinand lopez
i think that i should succeed in ascertain the extent of his capability and in make some use of they too wharton nearly ask his friend almost summon courage to ask him whether his father have do much for he
they be very intimate and on one subject in which lopez be much interested their confidence have be very close
but the young and the weak man of the two could not quite bring himself to the point of make an inquiry which he think would be disagreeable
lopez have never before in all their intercourse hint at the possibility of his have or have have filial aspiration
he have be as though he have be create self-sufficient independent of mother s milk or father s money
now the question might have be ask almost naturally
but it be not ask
everett wharton be a trouble to his father but not an agonizing trouble as be some son
his fault be not of a nature to rob his father s cup of all its sweetness and to bring his grey hair with sorrow to the grave
old wharton have never have to ask himself whether he should now at length let his son fall into the low abyss or whether he should yet again struggle to put he on his leg again forgive he again pay his debt again endeavour to forget dishonour and place it all to the score of thoughtless youth
have it be so i think that if not on the first or second fall certainly on the third the young man would have go into the abyss for mr
wharton be a stern man and capable of come to a clear conclusion on thing that be near and even dear to himself
but everett wharton have simply show himself to be inefficient to earn his own bread
he have never decline even to do this but have simply be inefficient
he have not declare either by word or action that as his father be a rich man and as he be an only son he would therefore do nothing
but he have try his hand thrice and in each case after but short trial have assure his father and his friend that the thing have not suit he
leave oxford without a degree for the reading of the school do not suit him he have go into a banking-house by no mean as a mere clerk but with an expressed proposition from his father back by the assent of a partner that he should work his way up to wealth and a great commercial position
but six month teach he that banking be an abomination and he at once go into a course of read with a barrister
he remain at this till he be called for a man may be call with very little continuous work
but after he be call the solitude of his chamber be too much for he and at twenty-five he find that the stock exchange be the mart in the world for such talent and energy as he possess
what be the nature of his failure during the year that he go into the city be know only to himself and his father unless ferdinand lopez know something of it also
but at six-and-twenty the stock exchange be also abandon and now at eight-and-twenty everett wharton have discover that a parliamentary career be that for which nature and his special genius have intend he
he have probably suggest this to his father and have meet with some cold rebuff
everett wharton be a good-look manly fellow six foot high with broad shoulder with light hair wear a large silky bushy beard which make he look old than his year who neither by his speech nor by his appearance would ever be take for a fool but who show by the very action of his body as well as by the play of his face that he lack firmness of purpose
he certainly be no fool
he have read much and though he generally forgot what he read there be leave with he from his reading certain nebulous light beget by other man s thinking which enable he to talk on most subject
it cannot be say of he that he do much think for himself but he think that he think
he believe of himself that he have go rather deep into politic and that he be entitle to call many statesmen ass because they do not see the thing which he see
he have the great question of labour and all that refer to union strike and lock-out quite at his finger end
he know how the church of england should be disestablish and recompose
he be quite clear on question of finance and see to a t how progress should be make towards communism so that no violence should disturb that progress and that in the due course of century all desire for personal property should be conquer and annihilate by a philanthropy so general as hardly to be account a virtue
in the meantime he could never contrive to pay his tailor s bill regularly out of the allowance of l400 a year which his father make he and be always dream of the comfort of a handsome income
he be a popular man certainly very popular with woman to whom he be always courteous and generally like by man to whom he be genial and good-natured
though he be not himself aware of the fact he be very dear to his father who in his own silent way almost admire and certainly like the openness and guileless freedom of a character which be very opposite to his own
the father though he have never say a word to flatter the son do in truth give his offspring credit for great talent than he possess and even when appear to scorn they would listen to the young man s diatribe almost with satisfaction
and everett be very dear also to a sister who be the only other live member of this branch of the wharton family
much will be say of she in these page and it be hop that the reader may take an interest in her fate
but here in speak of the brother it may suffice to say that the sister who be endow with infinitely fine gift than his do give credit to the somewhat pretentious claim of her less noble brother
indeed it have be perhaps a misfortune with everett wharton that some people have believe in him and a further misfortune that some other have think it worth their while to pretend to believe in he
among the latter might probably be reckon the friend with whom he be now din at the progress
a man may flatter another as lopez occasionally do flat wharton without preconcerted falsehood
it suit one man to be well with another and the one learn gradually and perhaps unconsciously the way to take advantage of the foible of the other
now it be most material to lopez that he should stand well with all the member of the wharton family as he aspire to the hand of the daughter of the house
of her regard he have already think himself nearly sure
of the father s sanction to such a marriage he have reason to be almost more than doubtful
but the brother be his friend and in such circumstance a man be almost justify in flatter a brother
iwill tell you what it be lopez say wharton as they stroll out of the club together a little after ten o clock the man of the present day willnot give themselves the trouble to occupy their mind with matter which have or should have real interest
pope know all about it when he say that the proper study of mankind be man but people donot read pope now or if they do they donot take the trouble to understand he man be too busy make money my dear fellow thatbe just it
moneybe a very nice thing very nice say lopez
but the search after it be debase
if a man could make money for four or six or even eight hour a day and then wash his mind of the pursuit as a clerk in an office wash the copy and ledger out of his mind then-- he would never make money in that way and keep it and therefore the whole thing be debase
a man cease to care for the great interest of the world or even to be aware of their existence when his whole soul be in spanish bond
they want to make a banker of i but i find that it would kill i it would kill i i think if i have to confine myself to spanish bond you know what i mean
you at any rate can understand i though i fear you be too far go to abandon the idea of make a fortune i would abandon it to-morrow if i could come into a fortune ready make
a man must at any rate eat yes he must eat
but i be not quite sure say wharton thoughtfully that he need think about what he eat unless the beef be send up without horse radish it have happen that when the two man sit down to their dinner the insufficient quantity of that vegetable supply by the steward of the club have be all consume and wharton have complain of the grievance
a man have a right to that for which he have pay say wharton with mock solemnity and if he pass over laches of that nature without observation he do an injury to humanity at large
ibe not go to be catch in a trap you know because i like horse radish with my beef
well i cannot go far out of my way as i have a deal of read to do before i court my morpheus
if youwill take my advice youwill go straight to the governor
whatever emily may feel i donot think shewill say much to encourage you unless you go about it after that fashion
she have prim notion of her own which perhaps be not after all so much amiss when a man want to marry a girl god forbid that i should think that anything about your sister be amiss i donot think there be much myself
woman be generally superficial but some be honestly superficial and some dishonestly
emily at any rate be honest stop half a moment then they saunter arm in arm down the broad pavement lead from pall mall to the duke of york s column
i wish i could make out your father more clearly
he be always civil to i but he have a cold way of look at i which make i think i be not in his good book he be like that to everybody i never seem to get beyond the skin with he
you must have hear he speak of i in my absence he never say very much about anybody but a word would let i know how the land lie
you know i well enough to be aware that i be the last man to be curious as to what other think of i
indeed i do not care about it as much as a man should do
i be utterly indifferent to the opinion of the world at large and would never object to the company of a pleasant person because the pleasant person abuse i behind my back
what i value be the pleasantness of the man and not his liking or dislike for myself
but here the dear aim of my life be concern and i might be guide either this way or that to my great advantage by know whether i stand well or ill with he you have din three time within the last three month in manchester square and i donot know any other man certainly no other young man who have have such strong proof of intimacy from my father yes and i know my advantage

the home rulers or irish party generally be leave without an inch of stand ground

for the moment there be not even a necessity to pretend that home rule be anything but an absurdity from begin to end so much so that one or two lead home rulers man who have take up the cause not only that they might become member of parliament but with some further idea of speech-making and popularity declare that the coalition have be form merely with a view of put down ireland
this capability of dispense with a generally untractable element of support be feel to be a great comfort
then too there be a set in the house at the moment not a very numerous set who have be troublesome friend to the old liberal party and which the coalition be able if not to ignore at any rate to disregard
these be the staunch economist and argumentative philosophical radicals man of stand and repute who be always in doubtful time individually flatter by ministers who have great privilege accord to they of speak and dividing and who be not unfrequently even thank for their rod by the very owner of the back which bear the scourge

gentleman bind to support the government when they perceive that there be comparatively but little to do and that that little might be easily do became careless and perhaps a little contemptuous
so that the great popular orator mr
turnbull find himself compel to rise in his seat and ask whether the noble duke at the head of the government think himself strong enough to rule without attention to parliamentary detail

chapter xxii st
james s park towards the end of september everett wharton and ferdinand lopez be in town together and as no one else be in town so at least they both profess to say they see a good deal of each other

chapter xxxiv the silverbridge election about a month after this affair with the runaway horse arthur fletcher go to greshamsbury preparatory to his final sojourn at silverbridge for the week previous to his election
greshamsbury the seat of francis gresham esq who be a great man in these part be about twenty mile from silverbridge and the tedious work of canvass the elector could not therefore be do from thence but he spend a couple of pleasant day with his old friend and learn what be be say and what be be do in and about the borough
mr
gresham be a man not as yet quite forty year of age very popular with a large family of great wealth and master of the county hound
his father have be an embarrassed man with a large estate but this gresham have marry a lady with immense wealth and have prosper in the world
he be not an active politician
he do not himself care for parliament or for the good thing which political power can give and be on this account averse to the coalition
he think that sir orlando drought and the other be touching pitch and have defile themselves
but he be conscious that in so think he be one of but a small minority and bad as the world around he certainly be terrible as have be the fall of the glory of old england he be nevertheless content to live without loud grumbling as long as the farmer pay he their rent and the labourer in his part of the country do not strike for wage and the land when sell would fetch thirty year purchase
he have not therefore be careful to ascertain that arthur fletcher would pledge himself to oppose the coalition before he proffer his assistance in this matter of the borough
it would not be easy to find such a candidate or perhaps possible to bring he in when find
the fletchers have always be good conservative and be proper people to be in parliament
a conservative in parliament be of course oblige to promote a great many thing which he do not really approve
mr
gresham quite understand that
you cannot have test and qualification rotten borough and the divine right of king back again
but as the glorious institution of the country be make to perish one after the other it be well that they should receive the coup de grace tenderly from love hand than be roughly throttle by radicals
mr

there be to be a third candidate

the duke s letter be address to his recognised man of business in those part and be as follows carlton terrace march 187
my dear mr
moreton mr
moreton be the successor of one mr
fothergill who have reign supreme in those part under the old duke i be afraid that my wish with regard to the borough and the forthcoming election there of a member of parliament be not yet clearly understand although i endeavour to declare they when i be at gatherum castle
i trust that no elector will vote for this or that gentleman with an idea that the return of any special candidate will please i
the ballot will of course prevent i or any other man from know how an elector may vote but i beg to assure the elector generally that should they think fit to return a member pledge to oppose the government of which i form a part it would not in any way change my cordial feeling towards the town
i may perhaps be allow to add that in my opinion no elector can do his duty except by voting for the candidate whom he think well qualified to serve the country
in regard to the gentleman who be now before the constituency i have no feeling for one rather than for the other and have i any such feeling i should not wish it to actuate the vote of a single elector
i should be glad if this letter could be publish so as to be bring under the eye of the elector generally
your faithfully omnium
when the duke say that he fear that his wish be not understand and speak of the inefficacy of his former declaration he be allude of course to the duchess and to mr

chapter xxxvii the horns the first month of the session go on very much as the last session have go

chapter xxxix get round him ferdinand lopez maintain his anger against his wife for more than a week after the scene at richmond feed it with reflection on what he call her disobedience
nor be it a make-believe anger
she have declare her intention to act in opposition to his express order
he feel that his present condition be prejudicial to his interest and that he must take his wife back into favour in order that he might make progress with her father but could hardly bring himself to swallow his wrath

nor on her behalf would he have hesitate though the misery might have continue for three month

wharton be a hale man with some terribly vexatious term of life before he

he would sit half bent in the arm-chair in stone buildings and look as though he be near a hundred

i be aware that i have no direct conversation with your grace on the subject and that your grace can plead that as between man and man i have no authority from yourself for suppose that i should receive your grace s support

but i must say with the most lively respect for your grace personally that i do not feel inclined to sit down tamely under such a loss as this

but i do it myself and i will abide by it
when he bid i go i shall go poor mr
wharton go to his chamber and sit there the whole day without take a book or a paper into his hand
could there be no rescue no protection no relief
he turn over in his head various plan but in a vague and useless manner
what if the duke be to prosecute lopez for the fraud
what if he could induce lopez to abandon his wife pledging himself by some deed not to return to her for say twenty or even thirty thousand pound
what if he himself be to carry his daughter away to the continent half force and half persuade she to make the journey
surely there might be some mean find by which the man might be frighten into compliance
but there he sat and do nothing
and in the evening he eat a solitary mutton chop at the jolly blackbird because he could not bear to face even his club and then return to his chambers to the great disgust of the old woman who have they in charge at night

nor could he see any remedy for his position

it be a nasty cold march night with a catch wind and occasional short shower of something between snow and rain as disagreeable a night for a gentleman to walk in as one could well conceive
but he go round by trafalgar square and along the strand and up some dirty street by the small theatre and so on to holborn and by bloomsbury square up to tottenham court road then through some unused street into portland place along the marylebone road and back to manchester square by baker street

he have trudge on and on with his umbrella over his head but almost unconscious of the cold and wet
and yet he be a man sedulously attentive to his own personal comfort and health who have at any rate show this virtue in his mode of living that he have never subject himself to danger by imprudence
but now the working of his mind keep he warm and if not dry at least indifferent to the damp
he have throw aside with affected nonchalance those question which his wife have ask he but still it be necessary that he should answer they
he do not suppose that he could continue to live in manchester square in his present condition

if he would consent to leave she his father-in-law would probably give he something some allowance on which he might exist
but then of what sort would be his life
he do not fail to remind himself over and over again that he have nearly succeed
he have be the guest of the prime minister and have be the nominee choose by a duchess to represent her husband s borough in parliament
he have be intimate with mills happerton who be fast become a millionaire
he have marry much above himself in every way
he have achieve a certain popularity and be conscious of intellect

he regard his fate as do a card-player who day after day hold six and seven when other man have the ace and king
fate be against he
he see no reason why he should not have have the ace and king continually especially as fate have give he perhaps more than his share of they at first
he have however lose rubber after rubber not pay his stake for some of the last rubber lost till the player would play with he no long
the misfortune might have happen to any man but it have happen to he
there be no beginning again

have lopez find his crossing at birmingham he would hardly have sweep it well

good-bye old girl he say donot be down-hearted if you have anything before you to do i will not be down-hearted she say
i shall have something to do before night i think
tell your father when you see he that i will not trouble he here much long
but tell he also that i have no thank to give he for his hospitality i will not tell he that ferdinand he shall know it though
but i do not mean to be cross to you
good-bye love then he stoop over she and kiss she again and so he take his leave of she
it be rain hard and when he get into the street he look about for a cab but there be none to be find
in baker street he get an omnibus which take he down to the underground railway and by that he go to gower street
through the rain he walk up to the euston station and there he order breakfast
could he have a mutton chop and some tea
and he be very particular that the mutton chop should be well cook
he be a good-look man of fashionable appearance and the young lady who attend he notice he and be courteous to he
he condescend even to have a little light conversation with she and on the whole he seem to enjoy his breakfast
upon my word i should like to breakfast here every day of my life he say
the young lady assure he that as far as she could see there be no objection to such an arrangement
only itbe a bore you know come out in the rain when there be no cab he say
then there be various little joke between they till the young lady be quite impressed with the gentleman s pleasant affability
after a while he go back into the hall and take a first-class return ticket not for birmingham but for the tenway junction
it be quite unnecessary to describe the tenway junction as everybody know it
from this spot some six or seven mile distant from london line diverge east west and north north-east and north-west round the metropolis in every direction and with direct communication with every other line in and out of london
it be a marvellous place quite unintelligible to the uninitiated and yet daily use by thousand who only know that when they get there they be to do what some one tell they
the space occupy by the convergent rail seem to be sufficient for a large farm
and these rail always run one into another with slop point and cross passage and mysterious meandering siding till it seem to the thoughtful stranger to be impossible that the well train engine should know its own line
here and there and around there be ever a wilderness of waggon some loaded some empty some smoking with close-pack oxen and other furlong in length black with coal which look as though they have be strand there by chance and be never destine to get again into the right path of traffic
not a minute pass without a train go here or there some rush by without notice tenway in the least crash through like flash of substantial lightning and other stop disgorging and take up passenger by the hundred
man and women especially the man for the woman know their ignorance be generally willing to trust to the pundit of the place look doubtful uneasy and bewilder
but they all do get properly place and unplace so that the spectator at last acknowledge that over all this apparent chaos there be preside a great genius of order
from dusky morn to dark night and indeed almost throughout the night the air be load with a succession of shriek
the theory go that each separate shriek if there can be any separation where the sound be so nearly continuous is a separate notice to separate ear of the coming or go of a separate train
the stranger as he speculate on these pandemoniac noise be able to realise the idea that be they discontinue the excitement necessary for the mind of the pundit might be lower and that activity might be lessen and evil result might follow
but he cannot bring himself to credit that theory of individual notice
at tenway junction there be half-a-dozen long platform on which man and woman and luggage be crowd
on one of these for a while ferdinand lopez walk backwards and forwards as though wait for the coming of some especial train
the crowd be ever so great that a man might be suppose to walk there from morning to night without exciting special notice
but the pundit be very clever and have much experience in man and woman
a well-teach pundit who have exercise authority for a year or two at such a station as that of tenway will know within a minute of the appearance of each stranger what be his purpose there whether he be go or have just come whether he be himself on the way or wait for other whether he should be treat with civility or with some curt command so that if his purport be honest all necessary assistance may be render he
as lopez be walk up and down with smile face and leisurely pace now read an advertisement and now watch the contortion of some amazed passenger a certain pundit ask he his business
he be wait he say for a train from liverpool intending when his friend arrive to go with he to dulwich by a train which go round the west of london
it be all feasible and the pundit tell he that the stop train from liverpool be due there in six minute but that the express from the north would pass first
lopez thank the pundit and give he sixpence which make the pundit suspicious
a pundit hop to be pay when he handle luggage but have no such expectation when he merely give information
the pundit still have his eye on our friend when the shriek and the whirr of the express from the north be hear
lopez walk quickly up towards the edge of the platform when the pundit follow he tell he that this be not his train
lopez then run a few yard along the platform not notice the man reach a spot that be unoccupied and there he stand fix
and as he stand the express flash by
i be fond of see they pass like that say lopez to the man who have follow he
but you shouldnot do it sir say the suspicious pundit
no one benot allow to stand near like that
the very hair of it might take you off your leg when yoube not use to it all right old fellow say lopez retreat
the next train be the liverpool train and it seem that our friend s friend have not come for when the liverpool passenger have clear themselves off he be still walk up and down the platform
hewill come by the next say lopez to the pundit who now follow he about and keep an eye on he
there benot another from liverpool stop here till the 2 20 say the pundit
you have well come again if you mean to meet he by that he have come on part of the way and will reach this by some other train say lopez
there benot nothing he can come by say the pundit
gentleman cannot wait here all day sir
the horder be against wait on the platform all right say lopez move away as though to make his exit through the station
now tenway junction be so big a place and so scatter that it be impossible that all the pundit should by any combined activity maintain to the letter that order of which our special pundit have speak
lopez depart from the platform which he have hitherto occupy be soon to be see on another walk up and down and again wait
but the old pundit have have his eye upon he and have follow he round
at that moment there come a shriek louder than all the other shriek and the morning express down from euston to inverness be see come round the curve at a thousand mile an hour
lopez turn round and look at it and again walk towards the edge of the platform
but now it be not exactly the edge that he near but a descent to a pathway an incline plane lead down to the level of the rail and make there for certain purpose of traffic
as he do so the pundit call to he and then make a rush at him for our friend s back be turn to the come train
but lopez heed not the call and the rush be too late
with quick but still with gentle and apparently unhurried step he walk down before the fly engine--and in a moment have be knock into bloody atom
chapter lxi the widow and her friend the catastrophe describe in the last chapter have take place during the first week in march
by the end of that month old mr

chapter lxii phineas finn have a book to read the sensation create by the man s death be by no mean confine to manchester square but be very general in the metropolis and indeed throughout the country

early in june have die the marquis of mount fidgett

it be admit to have be complete in the time of edward vi and be think to have be commence in the day of edward i

but now the prospect of that happiness have gradually vanish from he

the poison of place and power and dignity have get into his blood
as he look forward he fear rather than sigh for retirement
you think it will go against we he say
phineas do think so
there be hardly a man high up in the party who do not think so
when one branch of a coalition have gradually drop off the other branch will hardly flourish long
and then the tint of a political coalition be so neutral and unallure that man will only endure they when they feel that no more pronounced colour be within their reach
after all say phineas the innings have not be a bad one
it have be of service to the country and have last long than most man expect if it have be of service to the country that be everything
it should at least be everything
with the statesman to whom it be not everything there must be something wrong the duke as he say this be preach to himself
he be tell himself that though he see the well way he be allow himself to walk on in that which be bad
for it be not only phineas who could see the change or the old duke or the duchess
it be apparent to the man himself though he could not prevent it
i sometimes think he say that we whom chance have lead to be meddler in the game of politic sometimes give ourselves hardly time enough to think what we be about a man may have to work so hard say phineas that he have no time for think or more probably may be so eager in party conflict that he will hardly keep his mind cool enough for thought
it seem to i that many men man whom you and i know embrace the profession of politic not only without political conviction but without see that it be proper that they should entertain they
chance bring a young man under the guidance of this or that eld man
he have come of a whig family as be my case or from some old tory stock and loyalty keep he true to the interest which have first push he forward into the world
there be no conviction there conviction grow yes the conviction that it be the man s duty to be a staunch liberal but not the reason why
or a man see his opening on this side or on that as be the case with the lawyer
or he have a body of man at his back ready to support he on this side or on that as we see with commercial man
or perhaps he have some vague idea that aristocracy be pleasant and he become a conservative or that democracy be prosper and he become a liberal
you be a liberal mr
finn certainly duke why well after what you have say i will not boast of myself
experience however seem to show i that liberalism be demand by the country so perhaps at certain epoch may the devil and all his work but you will hardly say that you will carry the devil s colour because the country may like the devil
it be not sufficient i think to say that liberalism be demand
you should first know what liberalism mean and then assure yourself that the thing itself be good
i dare say you have do so but i see some who never make the inquiry i will not claim to be well than my neighbours i mean my real neighbour i understand i understand say the duke laughing
you prefer some good samaritan on the opposition bench to sir timothy and the pharisees
it be hard to come wound out of the fight and then to see he who should be your friend not only walk by on the other side but fling a stone at you as he go
but i do not mean just now to allude to the detail of recent misfortune though there be no one to whom i could do so more openly than to you
i be try yesterday to explain to myself why i have all my life sit on what be call the liberal side of the house to which i have belong do you succeed i begin life with the misfortune of a ready-make political creed
there be a seat in the house for i when i be twenty-one
nobody take the trouble to ask i my opinion
it be a matter of course that i should be a liberal
my uncle whom nothing could ever induce to move in politic himself take it for grant that i should run straight as he would have say
it be a tradition of the family and be as inseparable from it as any of the title which he have inherit
the property might be sell or squandered but the political creed be fix as adamant
i donot know that i ever have a wish to rebel but i think that i take it at first very much as a matter of course a man seldom inquire very deeply at twenty-one and if he do it be ten to one but he come to a wrong conclusion
but since then i have satisfy myself that chance put i into the right course
it have be i dare say the same with you as with i
we both go into office early and the anxiety to do special duty well probably deter we both from think much of the great question
when a man have to be on the alert to keep ireland quiet or to prevent peculation in the dockyard or to raise the revenue while he lower the taxis he feel himself to be save from the necessity of investigate principle
in this way i sometimes think that minister or they who have be minister and who have to watch minister from the opposition bench have less opportunity of become real politician than the man who sit in parliament with empty hand and with time at their own disposal
but when a man have be place by circumstance as i be now he do begin to think and yet you have not empty hand they be not so full perhaps as you think
at any rate i cannot content myself with a single branch of the public service as i use to do in old day
do not suppose that i claim to have make any grand political invention but i think that i have at least label my own thought
i suppose what we all desire be to improve the condition of the people by whom we be employ and to advance our country or at any rate to save it from retrogression that of course so much be of course
i give credit to my opponent in parliament for that desire quite as readily as i do to my colleague or to myself
the idea that political virtue be all on one side be both mischievous and absurd
we allow ourselves to talk in that way because indignation scorn and sometimes i fear vituperation be the fuel with which the necessary heat of debate be maintain there be some man who be very fond of poke the fire say phineas
well i willnot name any one at present say the duke but i have see gentleman of your country very handy with the poker phineas laugh know that he have be consider by some to have be a little violent when defend the duke
but we put all that aside when we really think and can give the conservative credit for philanthropy and patriotism as readily as the liberal
the conservative who have have any idea of the meaning of the name which he carry wish i suppose to maintain the difference and the distance which separate the highly place from their low brother
he think that god have divide the world as he find it divide and that he may well do his duty by make the inferior man happy and content in his position teach he that the place which he hold be his by god s ordinance and it be so hardly in the sense that i mean
but that be the great conservative lesson
that lesson seem to i to be hardly compatible with continual improvement in the condition of the low man
but with the conservative all such improvement be to be base on the idea of the maintenance of those distance
i as a duke be to be keep as far apart from the man who drive my horse as be my ancestor from the man who drive his or who ride after he to the wars and that be to go on for ever
there be much to be say for such a scheme
let the lord be all of they man with love heart and clear intellect and noble instinct and it be possible that they should use their power so beneficently as to spread happiness over the earth
it be one of the millennium which the mind of man can conceive and seem to be that which the conservative mind do conceive but the other man who be not lord donot want that kind of happiness if such happiness be attainable it might be well to constrain man to accept it
but the lord of this world be fallible man and though as unit they ought to be and perhaps be well than those other who have few advantage they be much more likely as unit to go astray in opinion than the body of man whom they would seek to govern
we know that power do corrupt and that we cannot trust king to have love heart and clear intellect and noble instinct
man as they come to think about it and to look forward and to look back will not believe in such a millennium as that do they believe in any millennium i think they do after a fashion and i think that i do myself
that be my idea of conservatism
the doctrine of liberalism be of course the reverse
the liberal if he have any fix idea at all must i think have conceive the idea of lessen distances of bring the coachman and the duke nearer together nearer and nearer till a millennium shall be reach by-- by equality ask phineas eagerly interrupt the prime minister and show his dissent by the tone of his voice
i do not use the word which be open to many objection
in the first place the millennium which i have perhaps rashly name be so distant that we need not even think of it as possible
man s intellect be at present so various that we cannot even realise the idea of equality and here in england we have be teach to hate the word by the evil effect of those absurd attempt which have be make elsewhere to proclaim it as a fact accomplish by the scratch of a pen or by a chisel on a stone
we have be injure in that because a good word signify a grand idea have be drive out of the vocabulary of good man
equality would be a heaven if we could attain it
how can we to whom so much have be give dare to think otherwise
how can you look at the bow back and bent leg and abject face of that poor ploughman who winter and summer have to drag his rheumatic limb to his work while you go a-hunting or sit in pride of place among the foremost few of your country and say that it all be as it ought to be
you be a liberal because you know that it be not all as it ought to be and because you would still march on to some near approach to equality though the thing itself be so great so glorious so godlike nay so absolutely divine that you have be disgust by the very promise of it because its perfection be unattainable

but through it all there be present to the heart of most of they a feeling that much more be to be effect if possible than this simple and cosy marriage and that the fate of mary wharton be hardly so important to they as that of emily lopez
when the carriage and four be go there come upon the household the difficulty usual on such occasion of get through the rest of the day
the bridesmaid retire and repacke their splendour so that they might come out fresh for other second-rate need and with the bridesmaid go the widow
arthur fletcher remain at wharton with all the other fletchers for the night and be prepared to renew his suit on that very day if an opportunity be give he but emily do not again show herself till a few minute before dinner and then she come down with all the appurtenance of mourning which she usually wear
the grey silk have be put on for the marriage ceremony and for that only
you should have keep your dress at any rate for the day say mrs
fletcher
she reply that she have change it for everett and that as everett be go there be no further need for she to wear clothe unfit to her position
arthur would have care very little for the clothe could he have have his way with the woman who wear them could he have have his way even so far as to have find himself alone with she for half-an-hour
but no such chance be his
she retreat from the party early and do not show herself on the follow morning till after he have start for longbarn
all the fletchers go back not however with any intention on the part of arthur to abandon his immediate attempt
the distance between the house be not so great but that he could drive himself over at any time
i shall go now he say to mr
wharton because i have promise john to fish with he to-morrow but i shall come over on monday or tuesday and stay till i go back to town
i hope she will at any rate let i speak to she the father say he would do his good but that that obstinate resumption of her weed on her brother s very wedding day have nearly break his heart
when the fletchers be back at longbarns the two lady be very severe on she
it be downright obstinacy say the squire s wife and it almost make i think it would serve she right to leave she as she be itbe pride say the old lady
she willnot give way
i say ever so much to her but itbe no use
i feel it the more because we have all go so much out of the way to be good to she after she have make such a fool of herself
if it go on much long i shall never forgive she again youwill have to forgive she mother say her eld son let she sin be what they may or else youwill have to quarrel with arthur i do think itbe very hard say the old lady take herself out of the room
and it be hard
the offence in the first instance have be very great and the forgiveness very difficult
but mrs
fletcher have live long enough to know that when son be thoroughly respectable a widow mother have to do their bidding
emily through the whole wedding day and the next day and day after day remember mrs
fletcher s word
there be some who will never be light-hearted again till they see you smile and the old woman have name her dearest friend and have end by name arthur fletcher
she have then acknowledge to herself that it be her duty to smile in order that other might smile also
but how be one to smile with a heavy heart
should one smile and lie
and how long and to what good purpose can such forced contentment last
she have mar her whole life
in former day she have be proud of all her virgin glories proud of her intellect proud of her beauty proud of that obeisance which beauty birth and intellect combine exact from all comer
she have be ambitious as to her future life had intend to be careful not to surrender herself to some empty fool had think herself well qualified to pick her own step
and this have come of it
they tell she that she might still make everything right annul the past and begin the world again as fresh as ever if she would only smile and study to forget
do it for the sake of other they say and then it will be do for yourself also
but she could not conquer the past
the fire and water of repentance adequate as they may be for eternity cannot burn out or wash away the remorse of this life
they scorch and choke and unless it be so there be no repentance
so she tell herself and yet it be her duty to be light-hearte that other around she might not be make miserable by her sorrow
if she could be in truth light-hearted then would she know herself to be unfeele and worthless
on the third day after the marriage arthur fletcher come back to wharton with the declare intention of remain there till the end of the holiday
she could make no objection to such an arrangement nor could she hasten her own return to london
that have be fix before her departure and be to be make together with her father
she feel that she be be attack with unfair weapon and that undue advantage be take of the sacrifice which she have make for her brother s sake
and yet yet how good to she they all be
how wonderful be it that after the thing she have do after the disgrace she have bring on herself and they after the destruction of all that pride which have once be her they should still wish to have she among they
as for him of whom she be always thinking of what nature must be his love when he be willing to take to himself as his wife such a thing as she have make herself
but think of this she would only tell herself that as he would not protect himself she be bind to be his protector
yes she would protect he though she could dream of a world of joy that might be hers if she could dare to do as he would ask she
he catch she at last and force she to come out with he into the ground
he could tell his tale well as he walk by her side than sit restlessly on a chair or move awkwardly about the room as on such an occasion he would be sure to do
within four wall she would have some advantage over he
she could sit still and be dignified in her stillness
but in the open air when they would both be on their leg she might not be so powerful with he and he perhaps might be strong with she
she could not refuse he when he ask she to walk with he
and why should she refuse he
of course he must be allow to utter his prayer and then she must be allow to make her answer
i think the marriage go off very well he say
very well
everett ought to be a happy man no doubt he will be when he settle down to something
everything will come right for he
with some people thing seem to go smooth donot they
they have not hitherto go smoothly with you and i emily you be prosperous
you have everything before you that a man can wish if only you will allow yourself to think so
your profession be successful and you be in parliament and everyone like you it be all nothing that be the general discontent of the world it be all nothing unless i have you too
remember that i have say so long before i be successful when i do not dream of parliament before we have hear of the name of the man who come between i and my happiness
i think i be entitle to be believe when i say so
i think i know my own mind
there be many man who would have be change by the episode of such a marriage you ought to have be change by it and by its result it have no such effect
here i be after it all tell you as i use to tell you before that i have to look to you for my happiness you should be ashamed to confess it arthur never not to you nor to all the world
i know what it have be
i know you be not now as you be then
you have be his wife and be now his widow that should be enough but such as you be my happiness be in your hand
if it be not so do you think that all my family as well as yours would join in wish that you may become my wife
there be nothing to conceal
when you marry that man you know what my mother think of it and what john think of it and his wife
they have want you to be my wife and they want it now because they be anxious for my happiness
and your father wish it and your brother wish it because they trust i and think that i should be a good husband to you good she exclaim hardly know what she mean by repeat the word
after that you have no right to set yourself up to judge what may be good for my happiness
they who know how to judge be all united
whatever you may have be they believe that it will be good for i that you should now be my wife
after that you must talk about i no long unless you will talk of my wish do you think i be not anxious for your happiness i do not know but i shall find out in time
that be what i have to say about myself
and as to you be it not much the same
i know you love i
whatever the feeling be that overcome you as to that other man it have go
i cannot now stop to be tender and soft in my word
the thing to be say be too serious to i
and every friend you have want you to marry the man you love and to put an end to the desolation which you have bring on yourself
there be not one among we all fletchers and whartons whose comfort do not more or less depend on your sacrifice the luxury of your own woe luxury yes luxury
no man ever have a right to say more positively to a woman that it be her duty to marry he than i have to you
and i do say it
i say it on behalf of all of we that it be your duty
i willnot talk of my own love now because you know it
you cannot doubt it
i willnot even talk of your because i be sure of it
but i say that it be your duty to give up drown we all in tear bury we in desolation
you be one of we and should do as all of we wish you
if indeed you could not love i it would be different
there
i have say what ihave get to say
you be cry and i will not take your answer now
i will come to you again to-morrow and then you shall answer i
but remember when you do so that the happiness of many people depend on what you say then he leave she very suddenly and hurry back to the house by himself
he have be very rough with her had not once attempt to touch she hand or even her arm have speak no soft word to she speak of his own love as a thing too certain to need further word and he have declare himself to be so assured of her love that there be no favour for he now to ask nothing for which he be bind to pray as a lover
all that be past
he have simply declare it to be her duty to marry he and have tell she so with much sternness
he have walk fast compel she to accompany he have frown at she and have more than once stamp his foot upon the ground
during the whole interview she have be so near to weep that she could hardly speak
once or twice she have almost think he to be cruel but he have force she to acknowledge to herself that all that he have say be true and unanswerable
have he press she for an answer at the moment she would not have know in what word to couch a refusal
and yet as she make her way alone back to the house she assure herself that she would have refuse
he have give she four-and-twenty hour and at the end of that time she would be bind to give he her answer an answer which must then be final
and as she say this to herself she find that she be admit a doubt
she hardly know how not to doubt know as she do that all whom she love be on one side while on the other be nothing but the stubbornness of her own conviction
but still the conviction be leave to she
over and over again she declare to herself that it be not fit mean thereby to assure herself that a high duty even than that which she owe to her friend demand from she that she should be true to her conviction
she meet he that day at dinner but he hardly speak to she
they sit together in the same room during the evening but she hardly once hear his voice
it seem to she that he avoid even look at she
when they separate for the night he part from she almost as though they have be stranger
surely he be angry with she because she be stubborn thought evil of she because she would not do as other wish she
she lie awake during the long night thinking of it all
if it might be so
oh if it might be so
if it might be do without utter ruin to her own self-respect as a woman
in the morning she be down early not have anything to say with no clear purpose as yet before her but still with a feeling that perhaps that morning might alter all thing for she
he be the late of the party not come in for prayer as do all the other but take his seat when the other have half finish their breakfast
as he sit down he give a general half-utter greeting to they all but speak no special word to any of they
it chance that his seat be next to her but to she he do not address himself at all
then the meal be over and the chair be withdraw and the party group itself about with vague uncertain movement as man and woman do before they leave the breakfast table for the work of the day
she meditate her escape but feel that she could not leave the room before lady wharton or mrs
fletcher who have remain at wharton to keep her mother company for a while
at last they went but then just as she be escape he put his hand upon she and remind she of her appointment
i shall be in the hall in a quarter of an hour he say
will you meet i there then she bow her head to he and pass on
she be there at the time name and find he stand by the hall door wait for she
his hat be already on his head and his back be almost turn to she
he open the door and allow she to pass out first lead the way to the shrubbery
he do not speak to she till he have close behind she the little iron gate which separate the walk from the garden and then he turn upon she with one word
well he say
she be silent for a moment and then he repeat his eager question well well i should disgrace you she say not firmly as before but whisper the word
he wait for no other assent
the form of the word tell he that he have win the day
in a moment his arm be round she and her veil be off and his lip be press to hers and when she could see his countenance the whole form of his face be alter to she
it be bright as it use to be bright in old day and he be smile on she as he use to smile
my own he said my wife--my own and she have no long the power to deny he
not yet arthur not yet be all that she could say
chapter lxxx the last meeting at matching the ex-prime minister do not carry out his purpose of leave london in the middle of the season and travel either to italy or norway
he be away from london at whitsuntide long perhaps than he might have be if still in office and during this period regard himself as a man from whose hand all work have be taken as one who have be find unfit to carry any long a burden serviceably but before june be over he and the duchess be back in london and gradually he allow himself to open his mouth on this or that subject in the house of lords not pitch into everybody all round as his wife have recommend but express an opinion now and again generally in support of his friend with the dignity which should belong to a retire prime minister
the duchess too recover much of her good temper as far at least as the outward show go
one or two who know she especially mrs
finn be aware that her hatred and her idea of revenge be not lay aside but she go on from day to day anathematize her special enemy and abstain from reproach her husband for his pusillanimity
then come the question as to the autumn
letus have everybody down at gatherum just as we have before say the duchess
the proposition almost take away the duke s breath
why do you want a crowd like that just to show they that we be not beat because we be turn out but inasmuch as we be turn out we be beat
and what have a gathering of people at my private house to do with a political manoeuvre
do you especially want to go to gatherum i hate the place
you know i do then why should you propose to go there he hardly yet know his wife well enough to understand that the suggestion have be a joke
if you donot wish to go abroad-- i hate go abroad then wewill remain at matching
you donot hate matching ah dear
there be memory there too
but you like it my book be there blue-book say the duchess
and there be plenty of room if you wish to have friend i suppose we must have somebody
you cannot live without your mentor you can ask whom you please he say almost fretfully
lady rosina of course suggest the duchess
then he turn to the paper before he and wouldnot say another word
the matter end in a party much as usual be collect at matching about the middle of october telemachus have spend the early part of the autumn with mentor at long royston

there be few there whom my patient reader have not see at matching before but among those few be arthur fletcher

she have by this time become intimate with her member as she would sometimes call he in joke and have concern herself much as to his matrimonial prospect
yes duchess it be to be unless some unforeseen circumstance should arise what circumstance lady and gentleman sometimes do change their minds but in this case i do not think it likely and why benot you be marry now mr
fletcher we have agree to postpone it till next year so that we may be quite sure of our own mind i know you be laugh at i but nevertheless i be very glad that it be settle

we get use to anything that chance send we in a marvellously short time what i mean be that i can go to bed and sleep and get up and eat my meal without miss the sound of the trumpet so much as i do at first
i remember hearing of people who live in a mill and couldnot sleep when the mill stop
it be like that with i when our mill stop at first
i have get myself so use to the excitement of it that i could hardly live without it you might have all the excitement still if you please
you need not be dead to politic because your husband be not prime minister no never again unless he should come back
if any one have tell i ten year ago that i should have take an interest in this or that man be in the government i should have laugh he to scorn
it do not seem possible to i then that i should care what become of such man as sir timothy beeswax and mr
roby
but i do get to be anxious about it when plantagenet be shift from one office to another of course you do
