i shall never forget what i feel when he come to i and tell i that perhaps it might be so but tell i also that he would escape from it if it be possible
i be the lady macbeth of the occasion all over wherea he be so scrupulous so burden with conscience
as for i i would have take it by any mean
then it be that the old duke play the part of the three witch to a nicety
well there havenot be any absolute murder and i havenot quite go mad nor need you be afraid though all the wood of gatherum should come to matching god forbid
i will never see anything of gatherum again
what annoy i most be and always be that he wouldnot understand what i feel about it how proud i be that he should be prime minister how anxious that he should be great and noble in his office how i work for he and not at all for any pleasure of my own i think he do feel it no not as i do
at last he like the power or rather fear the disgrace of lose it
but he have no idea of the personal grandeur of the place
he never understand that to be prime minister in england be as much as to be an emperor in france and much more than be president in america
oh how i do labour for him and how he do scold i for it with those quiet little stinging word of his
i be vulgar be that a quiet word yes as he use it and indiscreet and ignorant and stupid
i bear it all though sometimes i be die with vexation
now itbe all over and here we be as humdrum as any one else
and the beeswaxes and the robys and the drought and the pountneys and the lopezes have all pass over the scene
do you remember that pountney affair and how he turn the poor man out of the house it serve he right it would have serve they all right to be turn out only they be there for a purpose
i do like it in a way and it make i sad to think that the feeling can never come again
even if they should have he back again it would be a very lame affair to i then
i can never again rouse myself to the effort of prepare food and lodge for half the parliament and their wife
i shall never again think that i can help to rule england by coax unpleasant man
it be do and go and can never come back again not long after this the duke take mr
monk who have come down to matching for a few day out to the very spot on which he have sit when he indulge himself in lecture phineas finn on conservatism and liberalism generally and then ask the chancellor of the exchequer what he think of the present state of public affair
he himself have support mr
gresham s government and do not belong to it because he could not at present reconcile himself to fill any office
mr
monk do not scruple to say that in his opinion the present legitimate division of party be preferable to the coalition which have exist for three year
in such an arrangement say mr
monk there must always be a certain amount of distrust and such a feeling be fatal to any great work i think i distrust no one till separation came and when it do come it be not cause by i i be not blame any one now say the other but man who have be bring up with opinion altogether different even with different instinct as to politic who from their mother s milk have be nourish on code of thought altogether oppose to each other cannot work together with confidence even though they may desire the same thing
the very idea which be sweet as honey to the one be bitter as gall to the other you think then that we make a great mistake i will not say that say mr
monk
there be a difficulty at the time and that difficulty be overcome
the government be carry on and be on the whole respected
history will give you credit for patriotism patience and courage
no man could have do it well than you did probably no other man of the day so well but it be not a great part to play the duke in his nervousness as he say this could not avoid the use of that question tone which require an answer
great enough to satisfy the heart of a man who have fortify himself against the evil side of ambition
after all what be it that the prime minister of such a country as this should chiefly regard
be it not the prosperity of the country
it be not often that we want great measure or new arrangement that shall be vital to the country
politician now look for grievance not because the grievance be heavy but trust that the honour of abolish they may be great
it be the old story of the needy knife-grinder who if leave to himself would have no grievance of which to complain but there be grievance say the duke
look at monetary denomination
look at our weight and measure well yes
i will not say that everything have as yet be reduce to divine order
but when we take office three year ago we certainly do not intend to settle those difficulty no indeed say the duke sadly
but we do do all that we mean to do
for my own part there be only one thing in it that i regret and one only which you should regret also till you have resolve to remedy it what thing be that your own retirement from official life
if the country be to lose your service for the long course of year during which you will probably sit in parliament then i shall think that the country have lose more than it have gain by the coalition the duke sit for a while silent look at the view and before answer mr
monk while arrange his answer once or twice in a half-absent way call his companion s attention to the scene before he
but during this time he be go through an act of painful repentance
he be condemn himself for a word or two that have be ill-speak by himself and which since the moment of its utterance he have never cease to remember with shame
he tell himself now after his own secret fashion that he must do penance for these word by the humiliation of a direct contradiction of they
he must declare that caesar would at some future time be prepared to serve under pompey
then he make his answer
mr
monk he say i should be false if i be to deny that it please i to hear you say so
i have think much of all that for the last two or three month
you may probably have see that i be not a man endow with that fortitude which enable many to bear vexation with an easy spirit
i be give to fret and i be inclined to think that a popular minister in a free country should be so constitute as to be free from that infirmity
i shall certainly never desire to be at the head of a government again
for a few year i would prefer to remain out of office
but i will endeavour to look forward to a time when i may again perhaps be of some humble use
